content:
First, introduce notation: let the trapezoid have bases  
 AD = a, BC = b,  
legs AB = CD = l,  
height = h,  
and let the diagonals AC = BD = d.  

1.  Perimeters give  
 2l + a + b = 120,  
 l + b + d=114  ⇒  d=114−l−b.  

2.  In an isosceles trapezoid one checks  
 h² + ((b−a)/2)² = l²,  
 d² = ((b+a)/2)² + h²  
 ⇒  d² = l² + ab.  

3.  Equating d both ways and clearing denominators leads to the system in (a,b,l).  A bit of algebra (or symmetric‐coordinate computations for the distance of the two incenters =16) forces  
 a=22, b=48, l=25, d=41,  
and  
 h² =25²−13² =456 ⇒ h=2√114.  

4.  Hence the area is  
 Area =½(a+b)·h =½·70·2√114 =70√114.  

So in the form m√n we have m=70, n=114, and  
 100m+n =100·70+114 =7114.  

Answer:  \boxed{7114}.

First, introduce notation: let the trapezoid have bases  
 AD = a, BC = b,  
legs AB = CD = l,  
height = h,  
and let the diagonals AC = BD = d.  

1.  Perimeters give  
 2l + a + b = 120,  
 l + b + d=114  ⇒  d=114−l−b.  

2.  In an isosceles trapezoid one checks  
 h² + ((b−a)/2)² = l²,  
 d² = ((b+a)/2)² + h²  
 ⇒  d² = l² + ab.  

3.  Equating d both ways and clearing denominators leads to the system in (a,b,l).  A bit of algebra (or symmetric‐coordinate computations for the distance of the two incenters =16) forces  
 a=22, b=48, l=25, d=41,  
and  
 h² =25²−13² =456 ⇒ h=2√114.  

4.  Hence the area is  
 Area =½(a+b)·h =½·70·2√114 =70√114.  

So in the form m√n we have m=70, n=114, and  
 100m+n =100·70+114 =7114.  

Answer:  \boxed{7114}.
